title: Assessment of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency methods for identification of hazards to developing organisms, Part II: The developmental toxicity testing guideline
abstract:
Assessment of the U.S. Environmental Protection
Agency Methods for Identification of Hazards
to Developing Organisms, Part II:
The Developmental Toxicity Testing Guideline
Luz Claudio, PhD,1* Cynthia F. Bearer, MD, PhD,2 David Wallinga, MD3
Background The effects of toxins on developing animals depend not only on the nature of
the chemical but also on the timing of exposure and assessment of outcomes. This com-
plicates the task of regulatory agencies such as the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency
(EPA), which must comply with the 1996 Food Quality Protection Act to ensure that
their standards and policies protect infants and children from environmental toxins. For this
task, the Agency relies heavily on scientiﬁc data obtained by manufacturers of industrial
chemicals and pesticides following protocols collected under EPA’s Health Effects Test Guide-
lines.
Methods and Results This article reviews the protocols included in the EPA guidelines to
assess developmental toxicity, which are required for food-use pesticides under the core
testing battery. We reviewed these protocols on the basis of their adequacy for identifying
hazards to infants and children. Our analysis found limitations in the protocols that hinder
their potential for identifying developmental hazards.
Conclusions Methods that the EPA currently depends upon to identify developmental toxicity
of chemicals have limitations that impede obtaining complete and reliable data on which to
base regulatory decisions that protect children. Other methodological approaches need to be
explored as alternatives or supplements to the current protocols. Until more accurate testing
protocols become available, it may well be necessary under existing laws to employ safety
factors that are more protective of the health of children at all stages of development. Am. J.
Ind. Med. 35:554–563, 1999.
r 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
KEY WORDS: toxicity testing; developmental toxicity; environmental pollutants; prenatal
development; environmental protection agency; environmental health policy; environmen-
tal regulation; industrial chemicals; chemical safety; pesticides
INTRODUCTION
The National Research Council (NRC) published a
report in 1993 entitled Pesticides in the Diets of Infants and
Children, which established that children have greater
potential susceptibility to the toxic effects of pesticides due
to both their unique physiologic characteristics as well as
their higher proportional exposures to these chemicals.
Moreover, the NRC committee concluded that EPA’s then-
current testing protocols ‘‘[D]o not, for the most part,
adequately address the toxicity and metabolism of pesticides
1Division of Environmental and Occupational Medicine, Mount Sinai Medical
Center, New York, NY 10029–6574
2Department of Pediatrics, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, OH
44106
3Public Health Program, Natural Resources Defense Council, Washington, DC
20005
Contract grant sponsor: Natural Resources Defense Council; Contract grant
sponsor: Pew Charitable Trusts.
*Correspondence to: Luz Claudio, Division of Environmental and Occupational
Medicine, Mount Sinai School of Medicine, One Gustave L. Levy Place, New York,
NY 10029–6574. E-mail: Luz.Claudio@mssm.edu
Accepted 10 February 1999
AMERICAN JOURNAL OF INDUSTRIAL MEDICINE 35:554–563 (1999)
r 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
 in neonates and adolescent animals or the effects of exposure
during early developmental stages and their sequelae later
in life.’’ [National Research Council, 1993]. It has been
noted that toxicological data for considering risk to the
unborn and developing child are also lacking for many
important non-pesticide chemicals [Graeter and Mortensen,
1996].
While the NRC report established the scientiﬁc basis for
concern about the effects of pesticides on children’s health,
the Food Quality Protection Act (FQPA) sets the framework
for regulatory action. Under the FQPA, toxicological testing
in developing animals plays at least two major roles in
setting pesticide tolerances. First, in order to register a new
pesticide for use on food, EPA has a set of core toxicity
testing requirements (part of The Health Effects Test Guide-
lines, Series 870). These include testing for reproduction and
fertility in which some toxicological endpoints are assessed
in the offspring (reviewed in Part I of this report) and a
pre-natal developmental toxicity test (reviewed in this
report). These are the only two of the core tests that assess
effects in developing animals. Under the FQPA, EPA must
now reassess whether these tests would constitute a com-
plete database on which to base regulatory action. We ﬁnd
that the testing guidelines have deﬁciencies and limitations
that may reduce conﬁdence on their predictive value for
determining risk in children. Therefore, application of these
tests alone and in their present form would not constitute a
complete dataset that would justify the removal of the safety
factor required by the FQPA.
PRENATAL DEVELOPMENTAL TOXICITY
STUDY (OPPTS 870.3700 TSCA
799.9370)
Purpose and Principle
The Prenatal Developmental Toxicity Study is designed
to provide general information concerning effects of expo-
sure on the developing organism during the gestational
period. The Study consists of administering a test chemical
to pregnant animals at least from the time of implantation to
the end of the gestation period. The fetuses are analyzed for
visceral and skeletal malformations prior to their birth. The
test is designed to assess prenatal exposure and prenatal
outcomes exclusively. Its most important limitation is that it
does not expose or examine the experimental animals at any
time after birth. Although the EPA Health Effects Test
Guidelines include another test protocol that examines
animals during postnatal development [Preliminary Develop-
mental Toxicity Screen (OPPTS 870.3500, TSCA798.4420)],
this test is not part of the required core testing battery (see
Table I in the accompanying article). In practice, this test of
postnatal developmental toxicity is not always required by
EPA or is not conducted by manufacturers.
Test Animal Species and Strain
The guideline states that it is recommended that testing
be performed in the most relevant species. It does not
explain how the determination of relevance is to be made.
The rat and the rabbit may be used in these studies [Federal
Register, 1997]. Hamster and mouse are also acceptable
species in which to conduct developmental toxicity studies
and are occasionally used for this purpose [National Re-
search Council, 1993].
All the EPA Health Effects Testing Guidelines are based
on the notion that studies in laboratory animals can predict
toxicity in humans. For known human teratogens, this
statement is generally true. Most chemicals that have been
shown in human studies to cause developmental effects give
positive results when tested in laboratory animals. Few
studies have assessed comparisons between human and
animal sensitivity to developmental toxicants. In general, it
has been observed that humans are more sensitive to
developmental toxicants than the most sensitive test species
[Rogers and Kavlock, 1996]. For example, a comparative
study of the toxicity of thalidomide, methotrexate, valproic
acid, and isotretinoin found that human embryos were 0.9 to
10 times more sensitive than the most sensitive animal
species tested for these chemicals [Newman et al., 1993].
Another study showed that humans were 1.2 to 200 times
more sensitive to 19 of 21 teratogens tested in the most
sensitive laboratory species [Schardein and Keller, 1989].
These studies suggest that developmental effects can occur
at much lower exposures in humans than in the animals used
for testing developmental toxicity.
The gender of the laboratory animals being exposed to
the test substance may also inﬂuence the test results in
developmental toxicity studies. It has recently been recog-
nized that developmental effects on offspring may be
induced by exposure of the male parent to toxicants.
Epidemiological and experimental data have shown that
exposure of males to a number of agents including ionizing
radiation, and occupational and environmental exposures
can cause effects such as low birth weight, congenital
malformations, increased childhood cancers, and other devel-
opmental abnormalities in the offspring [Wyrobek, 1993].
Interestingly, many of these effects occur without lowering
the reproductive capacity of the exposed male, thus allowing
for the emergence of teratogenic effects in the resulting
progeny [Friedler, 1996]. Male-mediated developmental
effects on offspring are not considered in the current
guidelines for developmental toxicity testing, which expose
only pregnant females to the test substance. For the reasons
stated above, the choice of which species, strain, and gender
to use in testing possible developmental toxicants can be a
key determinant of test outcome.
555
Developmental Toxicity Test Guidelines

PRENATAL DEVELOPMENTAL TOXICITY
The Prenatal Developmental Toxicity Study is designed
to provide general information concerning effects of expo-
sure on the developing organism during the gestational
period. The Study consists of administering a test chemical
to pregnant animals at least from the time of implantation to
the end of the gestation period. The fetuses are analyzed for
visceral and skeletal malformations prior to their birth. The
test is designed to assess prenatal exposure and prenatal
outcomes exclusively. Its most important limitation is that it
does not expose or examine the experimental animals at any
time after birth. Although the EPA Health Effects Test
Guidelines include another test protocol that examines
animals during postnatal development [Preliminary Develop-
mental Toxicity Screen (OPPTS 870.3500, TSCA798.4420)],
this test is not part of the required core testing battery (see
Table I in the accompanying article). In practice, this test of
postnatal developmental toxicity is not always required by
EPA or is not conducted by manufacturers.
Test Animal Species and Strain
The guideline states that it is recommended that testing
be performed in the most relevant species. It does not
explain how the determination of relevance is to be made.
The rat and the rabbit may be used in these studies [Federal
Register, 1997]. Hamster and mouse are also acceptable
species in which to conduct developmental toxicity studies
and are occasionally used for this purpose [National Re-
All the EPA Health Effects Testing Guidelines are based
on the notion that studies in laboratory animals can predict
toxicity in humans. For known human teratogens, this
statement is generally true. Most chemicals that have been
shown in human studies to cause developmental effects give
positive results when tested in laboratory animals. Few
studies have assessed comparisons between human and
animal sensitivity to developmental toxicants. In general, it
has been observed that humans are more sensitive to
developmental toxicants than the most sensitive test species
[Rogers and Kavlock, 1996]. For example, a comparative
study of the toxicity of thalidomide, methotrexate, valproic
acid, and isotretinoin found that human embryos were 0.9 to
10 times more sensitive than the most sensitive animal
species tested for these chemicals [Newman et al., 1993].
Another study showed that humans were 1.2 to 200 times
more sensitive to 19 of 21 teratogens tested in the most
sensitive laboratory species [Schardein and Keller, 1989].
These studies suggest that developmental effects can occur
at much lower exposures in humans than in the animals used
for testing developmental toxicity.
The gender of the laboratory animals being exposed to
the test substance may also inﬂuence the test results in
developmental toxicity studies. It has recently been recog-
nized that developmental effects on offspring may be
induced by exposure of the male parent to toxicants.
Epidemiological and experimental data have shown that
exposure of males to a number of agents including ionizing
radiation, and occupational and environmental exposures
can cause effects such as low birth weight, congenital
malformations, increased childhood cancers, and other devel-
opmental abnormalities in the offspring [Wyrobek, 1993].
Interestingly, many of these effects occur without lowering
the reproductive capacity of the exposed male, thus allowing
for the emergence of teratogenic effects in the resulting
progeny [Friedler, 1996]. Male-mediated developmental
effects on offspring are not considered in the current
guidelines for developmental toxicity testing, which expose
only pregnant females to the test substance. For the reasons
stated above, the choice of which species, strain, and gender
to use in testing possible developmental toxicants can be a
key determinant of test outcome.
Developmental Toxicity Test Guidelines
It is stated in the guideline that: ‘‘At minimum, the test
substance should be administered daily from implantation to
cesarean section on the day prior to parturition. Alterna-
tively, if preliminary studies do not indicate a high potential
for pre-implantation loss, treatment may be extended to
include the entire period of gestation, from fertilization to
approximately 1 day before term.’’
This testing guideline is designed to assess developmen-
tal effects on the fetus only and should not be interpreted on
its own as indicative of possible adverse effects on infants
and children. For instance, it has been found that signiﬁcant
levels of toxic agents, including pesticides, can be present in
human breast milk [Rogan, 1996]. A classic study of the
postnatal effects of chlordane, for example, illustrates the
effects of exposure of the newborn through the maternal
milk. Rats exposed prenatally to chlordane showed in-
creased postnatal death. But when pregnant rats were
exposed to chlordane and their pups were then breast-fed by
un-exposed rats, the pups developed normally [Ingle, 1952].
This study and the later experiment by Narotsky and
Kavlock [1995] suggest that exposure through the lactation
period may cause signiﬁcant developmental effects in the
neonates, including postnatal death. Exposure to environmen-
tal toxicants during lactation can disrupt the normal develop-
ment of a baby because human infants continue to develop
postnatally. Therefore, the results obtained from this test should
be interpreted in conjunction with complementary tests that
assess effects in the postnatal animal. However, such tests are not
required under the core toxicity testing battery for food-use
pesticides, except as pertains to reproductive and fertility testing.
Outcome measures assessed in the guideline include
mortality, behavioral changes, and other signs of overt
toxicity, necropsy, and histopathologic examination in se-
lected dams and fetal animals. Mortality, moribundity,
pertinent behavioral changes, and all signs of overt toxicity
should be recorded at the time of cageside observation of the
pregnant animals. In addition, thorough physical examina-
tions should be conducted at the same time and maternal
body weights recorded. The day prior to the scheduled day
of parturition, the fetuses are extracted by cesarean section
and the females are terminated.
Only the uteri that appear non-gravid are required to be
examined by ammonium sulﬁde staining. The technique
consists of immersing the uteri in a 10% solution of
ammonium sulﬁde. If the uteri are not then counterstained,
the implantation sites will appear stained blue-black whereas
the surrounding uterine tissue will appear light green as
described in Weir et al. [1987] and in Kelich et al. [1995].
This technique is used to distinguish animals that did not
become pregnant after mating from those in which full litter
resorption occurred. This is an important distinction because
full litter resorption indicates loss of pregnancy and death of
The technique of staining for resorption sites with
ammonium sulﬁde has been recently reassessed [Narotsky et
al., 1997]. These authors suggest that when the time of the
staining procedure is extended from the usual 15 minutes to
between 1 and 4.5 hours, the detection rate of full litter
resorptions is increased. It may be relevant to make this
change in the current protocol, especially when assessing the
effects of toxins that are likely to cause prenatal death.
The guideline only requires that uteri that appear
non-gravid need to be examined by the ammonium sulﬁde
method. Since some toxicants may induce only partial
resorption of the litter, it may therefore be advisable that the
staining procedure be conducted in gravid uteri after the
removal of attached fetuses. The staining of all uteri,
regardless of the apparent state of pregnancy, may be
appropriate to more accurately distinguish between pre- and
early post-implantation fetal death. Narotsky and coworkers
utilized a classiﬁcation scale for the levels of fetal death in
the uteri [Narotsky et al., 1997]. In this method, each
implantation site was classiﬁed as a live or dead fetus or a
resorption site that is then rated according to degree of
scariﬁcation. The length of the scar can give an indication of
the time of death of the conceptus.
Because of normal variation in litter size and birth
weight among animals, the studies as designed in the
guidelines can only detect relatively large effects of the test
compounds. For example, it has been calculated that to
detect a 5% increase in embryonic resorptions induced by
the pesticide 2,4,5-T it would be necessary to utilize 805
litters per dose [Nelson and Holson, 1978]. To enhance the
statistical power of developmental studies, it has been pro-
posed that approaches utilizing correlated binary data analy-
sis be employed [Ten-Have and Hartzel, 1995]. However,
these methods have not been validated for use in the
The guideline requires that the sex and body weight of
each fetus should be determined. Each fetus should be
examined for external anomalies as well. For rodents, it is
recommended that half of each litter be prepared for
assessment of skeletal malformations and the other half for
soft tissue anomalies. Alternatively (and for rabbits), both
parameters may be examined in the same fetuses. Simulta-
neous staining of skeletal elements and observation of
viscera can be done using the method of Monie et al. [1965],
which is referenced in the guideline.
The method cited for detection of visceral malforma-
tions is a free hand serial sectioning of the head, neck, and
lower trunk using a razor blade [Barrow and Taylor, 1969].
The sections are made by following anatomical landmarks
sequentially on the fetus in order to obtain visualization of
the various organs at desired levels. These sections serve
primarily to detect cleft palate, abnormalities of the eyes,
internal hydrocephalus, and some heart malformations.
Gross malformations of organs such as the lungs, thymus
gland, liver, stomach, pancreas, spleen, adrenals, and the
great vessels may also be visible by this method of
dissection. Some defects may not be recognized by using
this method such as defects of the interatrial septum and the
atrioventricular valves of the heart. However, this method
has the advantage that it is fast and relatively easy to
The extent of prenatal ossiﬁcation in rodents and rabbits
has been proposed as an indication of degree of maturity of
the fetus and thus is included in the Prenatal Developmental
Toxicity Study as a primary endpoint [Spark and Dawson,
1928; Strong, 1928; Fritz and Hess, 1970]. The bone can be
distinguished from the cartilaginous skeleton by using a
double-staining technique [Igarashi et al., 1992]. This tech-
nique consists of the differential staining with both alcian
blue (cartilage) and alizarin red S (bone) stains. After
studying normal and abnormal ossiﬁcation in neonatal mice
and rats, investigators concluded that the state of ossiﬁcation
of the axial skeleton at birth can provide an indication of the
degree of developmental retardation.
In addition to degree of ossiﬁcation, skeletal malforma-
tions are measured as an indicator of developmental toxicity.
One disadvantage of using skeletal malformations as an
indicator is the relatively high frequency of spontaneous
malformations that occur in un-exposed animals. For ex-
ample, one study found 88 types of spontaneous skeletal
variations in CD-1 mice [Beck, 1981]. The study by Igarashi
et al. [1992], which details the recommended technique for
double staining the skeletal elements, found ﬁfteen types of
skeletal variations. Further, these authors found variations in
the incidence of skeletal malformations in rats of two
strains: the CD and the Wistar rat. Even authors cited in the
guideline as sources of information regarding techniques for
assessing skeletal elements question whether the many
variations can be interpreted as indicative of malformations
due to the chemical treatment being tested [Kimmel and
Wilson, 1973]. Those authors concluded that none of the
skeletal variants that they found in 20-day rat fetuses could
in themselves be considered malformations; rather, these
variations can only be classiﬁed as abnormal when they are
accompanied by other signs of embryotoxicity.
The signiﬁcance of these skeletal abnormalities are best
observed several days after birth [Fritz and Hess, 1970]. For
example, rats exposed to ethylene glycol during gestational
days 6 through 15 exhibit skeletal abnormalities until
postnatal day 21. However, animals examined at postnatal
day 63 do not continue to show these abnormalities,
suggesting that the perinatal skeletal abnormalities observed
may not always persist in the exposed animals [Marr et al.,
Some developmental effects have been shown to be
observed only at later stages in the postnatal development
period. For example, heptachlor can cause increased postna-
tal death (day 6 and later) after prenatal exposure. It has also
been observed that prenatal exposure to heptachlor can
produce the advent of ocular cataracts that do not become
apparent until postnatal day 21 [Mestitzova, 1967; Narotsky
and Kavlock, 1995]. This illustrates the possibility that
developmental outcomes can be missed if the observation
period is limited to the prenatal or neonatal periods.
The Prenatal Developmental Toxicity Study, as the title
implies, does not measure any effects on animals after birth.
This limits the value of the test greatly because several
characteristics of the newborn animal can make it distinct
from the fetal animal harvested even just before birth. One
example of this is the state of ossiﬁcation of the animal,
which, as described above, is one of the chief developmental
characteristics evaluated. Of the tests included in the re-
quired battery for food-use pesticides, only the Reproductive
and Fertility test (reviewed in Part I) assesses effects of
prenatal exposure postnatally. However, the test is not
designed to assess important developmental effects which
are included in the Developmental Guideline.
for Developmental Toxicity Testing
Current developmental toxicity testing guidelines pro-
vide some predictive value towards identifying possible
human teratogens. As is suggested by the limitations de-
scribed above, however, even the most predictive animal
models are not wholly reliable individually. For chemicals
identiﬁed as known human teratogens, calculations range
from 6 to 15% of the tests in animals producing false-
positive results and from 11 to 29% of tests producing
false-negative results [Schardein et al., 1989]. This and other
studies suggest the need for the development and validation
of new more predictive tests for teratogenicity. Below are
some alternative approaches that have been proposed for
screening and testing of developmental toxicants.
Structure-activity relationships
Because of the time and expense needed for testing the
thousands of chemicals that require evaluation of their toxic
properties, regulatory agencies have searched for alternative
models for predicting toxicity. Structure-activity relation-
ships (SARs) are used to some extent for priority setting in
the regulatory process. These decisions may be based on
various aspects of the compound in question such as
Developmental Toxicity Test Guidelines
chemical class, structure, stability, solubility, pH, electro-
philicity, lipophilicity, reactivity, and the presence of struc-
tural moieties that are known to have some biological effect
[Faustman and Omenn, 1996]. This approach has been used
most commonly for the regulation of potential carcinogens.
The use of SARs and quantitative SARs (QSARs) for
assessing developmental toxicity has been limited by the
lack of a large database of published developmental toxicol-
ogy data on which to base the predictions. The development
of SARs requires the use of subsets of active and inactive
congeners from multiple chemical classes in order to
validate the strategy used for the determination of those
SARs and to determine the ability of a particular test to
predict effects of a chemical on development [Kavlock,
1993]. One example of the complexity of determining
QSARs in developmental toxicology was the comparison of
SARs for phenols determined in vitro and in vivo [Kavlock,
1990]. This study found that the properties of the phenols
tested that produced maternal toxicity, including lipophilic-
ity and electron withdrawing ability, were different from
those related to the developmental toxicity of the chemicals.
There were also a number of inconsistencies between the in
vivo effects of phenols as compared to their effects in in vitro
assays, which may have been related to the metabolic
capacity provided by the mother. In addition, differences in
species sensitivity may enhance the predictive value of the
SARs in some species and reduce it in others [Kavlock et al.,
Attempts have been made to produce computerized
systems for predicting developmental toxicity using existing
databases of the effects of various chemical classes. One
such computer program designated CASE for Computer
Automated Structure Evaluation has been tested for the
assessment of developmental toxicity [Takihi et al., 1994].
This system uses a database of known teratogens and
analyzes their chemical structure and biological effects in
order to obtain a list of molecular fragments that may confer
toxic activity or inactivity of the substance. Other chemicals
can then be scanned for the presence of the molecular
fragments that may predict their developmental effects. By
using randomly constructed learner and tester sets from a
database consisting of 125 chemicals of known developmen-
tal toxicity in mice, rats, rabbits, and humans, the authors
obtained a concordance level of predictive value of between
Although this level of accuracy in the predictive value
of CASE is not acceptable as a basis for the determination of
developmental toxicity potential, improvements are being
made in the area of computerized toxicity assessment using
QSARs. Another similar computerized approach named
TOPKAT has been tested for its value in assessing develop-
mental toxicity [Gombar et al., 1995]. The computer model
was applied for aliphatic, heteroaromatic, and carboaromatic
compounds. Through a cross-validation procedure, it was
found that the model could reach speciﬁcities of 86 to 97%
and sensitivities between 86 and 89%.
Clearly, more work is needed to design computer
models that can be applied more broadly to predictions of
developmental toxicity effects for chemicals of unknown
activity. However, the validity of SARs for predicting
developmental toxicity has been questioned [Kolb-Meyers
and Beyler, 1981; Schardein, 1993]. It is most likely that
these models may become useful for determining develop-
mental hazards within a group of chemicals with structural
commonalties, but predictions outside the chemical sub-
group would not be valid. This conclusion can be illustrated
by the ﬁndings of early investigations of the validity of SAR,
which found that of the 527 teratogens listed in the Registry
of Toxic Effects of Chemical Substances of 1979, only 35
had chemical structures in common [Kolb-Meyers and
Beyler, 1981]. Therefore, the limitations that SARs present
for the identiﬁcation of compounds of unknown developmen-
tal toxicity severely hinder their application in the regulatory
process. The SARs may be somewhat useful, however, as an
adjunct tool for certain chemical groups with common
Embryos and organs set in culture can be used as
facsimiles of the developmental events that occur in the
intact organism. These methods, if validated and designed
appropriately, have the potential to serve as powerful tools
for screening and perhaps identifying developmental toxi-
cants. However, most of these methods are criticized be-
cause they dissociate the organism and disrupt the normal
interactions that exist between the maternal and the embry-
onic milieu. Organotypic systems, which place only portions
of the embryo in culture, have the additional disadvantage of
further dissociating the dynamic contributions of different
organic systems such as the circulatory and immune sys-
tems. Further, in vitro systems can be technically demand-
ing, which makes them relatively expensive.
On the positive side, culture systems have the advantage
of potentially giving results in a shorter amount of time and
are ideal tools for obtaining data on mechanisms of action of
chemicals. Various types of culture systems have been used
for the assessment of developmental toxicity.
In this method, the embryos are dissected free of uterine
tissue while leaving the ectoplacental cone and yolk sac
intact, and placed in culture dishes where chemicals can be
tested directly. Embryo cultures have been utilized with
some success for the study of various teratogens [Webster et
al., 1997]. For example, a study of various enantiomers of
valproic acid (a teratogenic antiepileptic drug) on cultured
mouse embryos showed that the in vitro system could
distinguish those molecules that were known to be terato-
genic in vivo from those which were not [Andrews et al.,
1995, 1997]. Another study utilized rat whole embryo
cultures to study the teratogenicity of 13 different phenols
[Oglesby et al., 1992]. The investigators found that all of the
phenols tested produced effects on growth and morphogen-
One of the advantages of using whole embryo cultures
is that it is possible to distinguish true developmental
toxicity from secondary effects on the embryo due to
maternal toxicity. Because regulatory testing guidelines
often require dosing to occur at levels that induce overt
toxicity on the pregnant animals, it is often not possible to
determine whether the test chemical constitutes a speciﬁc
threat to the developing fetus. This distinction has become
controversial in deﬁning what designates a chemical to be a
teratogen [Schardein, 1993]. The whole embryo culture
techniques can also help elucidate mechanisms of action and
active metabolites of a toxicant as in the case of developmen-
tal toxicity of ethylene glycol [Carney et al., 1996].
To mimic the contribution of maternal metabolism to
the teratogenic potential of a chemical, investigators have
designed coculture systems that include maternal hepatic
fractions with the whole embryo cultures. For example,
maternal hepatocytes were necessary for the expression of
concentration-dependent cyclophosphamide embryotoxicity
[Ozolins et al., 1995], while coculture with hepatocytes
reduced the toxic effect observed in cultured embryos
exposed to trichloroethylene or tetrachloroethylene [Saillen-
fait et al., 1995], and it did not have a measurable effect on
the stereoselective teratogenicity elicited by valproic acid
enantiomers [Andrews et al., 1997]. Therefore, the need for
using metabolizing hepatocytes in coculture with whole embryo
cultures depends on the nature of the chemical being tested.
Another in vitro approach involves the culture of
portions of embryonic tissues on which toxicity may be
suspected. One organ that is often assessed by using these
models is the brain. Explants from brain and spinal cord can
be cultured in vitro to obtain integrated neuronal and glial
populations that retain many characteristics of the tissue in
situ. The tissue is obtained from undifferentiated embryonic
rodents [Studer et al., 1994] chicks [Daston et al., 1995,
Yamagata et al., 1995], or humans [Hatch et al., 1994].
Under optimal conditions, these tissues can undergo axonal
and dendritic growth, myelination, and synaptogenesis in
culture. These parameters can be monitored by light micros-
copy with little disturbance to the culture. Another advan-
tage of organotypic cultures is their longevity, since it is
possible to maintain a culture for up to a year. This feature
makes the technique especially suitable for the study of
An organotypic system that has been used successfully
is the preparation of the sensory-motor unit. By preparing a
co-culture of spinal cord, ganglia, and muscle, investigators
have been able to produce a representation of the sensory-
motor unit in vitro, which contains elements of the central
and peripheral nervous systems. This makes the preparation
especially suitable for testing of substances that may act on
either part of the neuraxis. Some of the compounds tested so
far with this type of organotypic culture include triethyltin
[Graham et al., 1975], thallium [Spencer et al., 1973],
methyl n-butyl ketone [Veronesi et al., 1980], pesticides
[Tuler et al., 1989], and others.
Other organotypic culture models have also been used
to try to obtain data that can be applied as representative of
the toxic effects on the whole organism. Notably, the limb
bud cell culture system has been used for assessment of
developmental toxicity [Flint et al., 1984]. The principle of
this model is that rodent embryo limb bud cells multiply and
undergo chondrogenesis in micromass culture. Disruption of
this process by adding toxic substances to the culture media
can then be quantiﬁed by determination of proteoglycan
production, or by determination of cartilaginous foci num-
ber, which are indications of the degree of inhibition of
An improved version of the limb bud cell culture
teratogenicity test was advanced by Renault et al. [1989]. In
this method, the parameters of cell proliferation and cell
differentiation are distinguished to determine the speciﬁc
teratogenic action of the test chemical. This gave the method
a sensitivity of 61% with a speciﬁcity of 100% because of
the reduction of false-positive outcomes that were common
with this protocol. The ﬁnal accuracy of the method,
however, was 75% when validated using a list of 51
chemicals (33 known teratogens and 18 nonteratogens). The
limb bud cell culture method has been used particularly for
the study of agents that may inhibit the normal development
of the fetal extremities or may cause other types of skeletal
malformations [Tsuiki et al., 1996; Pignatello et al., 1997].
More validation work would be needed to investigate the
applicability of this model to general teratogenicity testing
Frog embryo teratogenesis assay-xenopus
The Frog Embryo Teratogenesis Assay-Xenopus, better
known as FETAX, is another in vitro test that has been
proposed speciﬁcally for the testing of developmental toxic-
ity. It consists of a 96-hour whole embryo developmental
toxicity test that utilizes the Xenopus laevis, the South
A three-phase multicenter protocol has been designed to
validate FETAX as a possible developmental toxicity assay
[Bantle et al., 1994, 1996]. This validation procedure
consists of Phase I, a training protocol and evaluation phase
in which the identity of the three test materials (hydroxy-
Developmental Toxicity Test Guidelines
urea, isoniazid and 6-aminonicotinamide) was known. Phase
II was designed in a similar fashion as Phase I, except that
the identity of the test materials was not known. In Phase III,
investigators tested FETAX in a blind testing format with
each laboratory responsible for determining the concentra-
tions to be tested. The major parameters to be assessed in
this seven-center study was the repeatability, reliability, and
reproducibility of the test results within laboratories and
between the participating laboratories. The results showed
that the main causes for variation in the FETAX protocol
appeared to be the subjective judgment of the technicians
asked to evaluate whether malformations were apparent in
theembryos.Additionofareferencetoxicantsuchas6-aminonic-
otinamide can improve the reliability of the test results, but so far
further work is needed to improve the use of this test as a possible
screening method for unknown teratogenic chemicals.
The FETAX system has been used to assess the
developmental toxicity of various chemicals including pesti-
cides atrazine and 2,4-D [Morgan et al., 1996], the pesticide
Guthion [Schuytema et al., 1994], metals [Plowman et al.,
1994], and industrial chemicals [Fort et al., 1996a,b]. The
method has been modiﬁed to allow for longer exposure time
and has been applied to evaluate the effects of 1-heptanol on
mortality, malformation and growth inhibition [Bernardini et
al., 1994]. The method can be further manipulated to include
a metabolic activation system consisting of hepatic cells
composed of rat liver microsomes to mimic the potential
detoxifying or potentiating activity that can result from liver
metabolism [Fort et al., 1993; Bantle et al., 1996].
General risk assessment procedures have been applied
previously to the identiﬁcation of developmental toxicants
in humans [EPA, 1986; Jelovsek et al., 1989]. These
procedures include: hazard identiﬁcation, hazard character-
ization, exposure assessment, and risk characterization. Yet
risk assessment for developmental toxicity based on current
testing requirements for chemicals has led to relatively few
chemical agents being recognized as human teratogens. Of
hundreds of chemicals that have been shown in the scientiﬁc
literature to produce developmental effects in animals, only
a small proportion has been classiﬁed as toxic to human
development [Schardein and Keller, 1989; Newman et al.,
The paucity of the identiﬁcation of developmental
toxins illustrates the dichotomy that although all chemicals
that have been found to be teratogenic in humans have been
conﬁrmed teratogenic in laboratory animals, the converse
statement has not been proven true; i.e., not all chemicals
that have been shown to be teratogenic in animals have been
proven so in epidemiological or case studies of humans
[Schardein, 1993]. This is partly because data on human
exposures is scarce for most chemicals. Even where it exists,
confounding factors such as genetic variability in the
populations, nutritional status, exposure to multiple agents,
low statistical power of human epidemiological studies, and
other factors make it difficult to ascribe effects of chemicals
to speciﬁc human endpoints [Schardein et al., 1985]. It is for
this reason that the regulatory process must largely rely on
laboratory tests in animals to determine developmental
The limitations we have found in the Prenatal Develop-
mental Toxicity Study protocols are summarized in Table I.
One of the most important limitations of current test
protocols for identifying developmental toxicants is that
they fail to reﬂect the full range of possible exposure
scenarios and health outcomes that can occur in developing
TABLE I. Summary of Limitations in the Developmental Toxicity Test Guideline

and non-rodent) are usually recommended,
most often rabbit and rat or mouse
Specify that the most sensitive laboratory
animal species must be used since humans
appear to be the most sensitive species
Only exposes pregnant female animals
Consider male-mediated developmental effects
by exposing male animals [Friedler, 1996]
Exposure through the length of gestational
Extend to include perinatal exposure (i.e., lac-
tation) [Narotski and Kavlock, 1995]
Three doses: high causes maternal toxicity,
medium has minimal effects, low has no
May need pharmacokinectic data prior to the
developmental assessment [EPA Report
Staining of only non-gravid uteri
Increase detection of embryonic resorptions
Macroscopic visceral and skeletal examination
Variability of skeletal malformations needs
humans. Speciﬁcally, EPA’s Developmental Toxicity Test
Guideline limits exposure and outcome measures to pre-
natal stages of development, which may be inadequate
for uncovering effects that arise after birth. For example, it
would be rare for a pregnant woman to receive exposure
to a particular compound only during a speciﬁc period of
gestation such as organogenesis. It is more likely that hu-
man exposures will occur at constant or sporadic episodes
throughout gestation and lactation and occasional acute or
chronic episodes of exposure may occur when occupational
exposures are involved [Garcia, 1998]. Therefore, it is
important that the protocol be used in conjunction with
other tests that include assessment of toxicity during the
postnatal developmental stages. The Reproductive and
Fertility test does assess some developmental endpoints
in postnatal animals, but its focus is really the mature
animal and it does not assess all developmental endpoints of
interest (see accompanying article). Other speciﬁc limi-
tations of the current testing protocol, examined earlier,
●species, strain, and gender of animal exposed may not be
as sensitive as humans to developmental toxins
●protocols do not test for male-mediated effects on off-
●does not require examination to assess partial resorption
●reliance on degree of ossiﬁcation and skeletal malforma-
tions as primary endpoints to deﬁne fetal toxicity
Since the passage of the Food Quality Protection Act,
the need for better testing methods for developmental
toxicity has become quite relevant. Some alternative or
complementary methods that could be explored to improve
the testing guideline are included in this article. Until better
methods are designed, tested, and validated, however, it may
well be necessary to err on the side of safety to protect the
unborn and the young from developmental toxins, especially
in light of the legislative mandates of the FQPA and the 1997
Presidential Executive Order on Children’s Environmental
Health and Safety Risks. Given the state of the art in
developmental toxicology, it will be necessary for regulatory
agencies to accept a level of uncertainty that is consistent
with the lack of knowledge and the limitations of the current
developmental testing guidelines. This includes applying
safety factors to the risk assessment of chemicals that is
reﬂective of these limitations.
